**Gush Dan** ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): גּוּשׁ דָּן, lit. 'Dan bloc') or **Tel Aviv metropolitan area** is a major [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") along [Israel's](/wiki/Israel "Israel") [Mediterranean coast](/wiki/Israeli_coastal_plain "Israeli coastal plain"). The term is commonly used by government bodies and the public, though its exact boundaries vary. It ranges from combining [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") with cities that form an urban continuum with it, to the entire areas from both the [Tel Aviv District](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_District "Tel Aviv District") and the [Central District](/wiki/Central_District_(Israel) "Central District (Israel)"), or sometimes the whole [Metropolitan Area](/wiki/Metropolitan_Area "Metropolitan Area") of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"),[[2]](#cite_note-cbs.gov.il-2) which includes a small part of the [Southern District](/wiki/Southern_District_(Israel) "Southern District (Israel)") as well. Gush Dan is the largest conurbation and metropolitan area in Israel and the center of Israel's [financial](/wiki/Economy_of_Israel "Economy of Israel") and [High technology](/wiki/Silicon_Wadi "Silicon Wadi") sector. In 2021 the metropolitan area has an estimated population of 4,156,900 residents, 89% of whom were [Israeli Jews](/wiki/Israeli_Jews "Israeli Jews").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gush_Dan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Historical population

| Year | Pop. | ±% |
| --- | --- | --- |

The name *Gush Dan* means "Dan Bloc", and is so named because the area was the territory of the [tribe of Dan](/wiki/Tribe_of_Dan "Tribe of Dan") in the ancient [Kingdom of Israel](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Israel_(united_monarchy) "Kingdom of Israel (united monarchy)"). According to the biblical narrative, the tribe had originally tried to settle in the central coastal area of [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan "Canaan"), but enmity with the [Philistines](/wiki/Philistines "Philistines"), who had already settled there, caused it to be able to camp only in the hill country overlooking the [Sorek Valley](/wiki/Sorek_Valley "Sorek Valley"). The camp location became known as Mahaneh Dan ("Camps of Dan"). The region that they attempted to settle included the area as far north as [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa") and as far south as [Shephelah](/wiki/Shephelah "Shephelah") in the area of [Timnah](/wiki/Timnah "Timnah"). As a result of the pressure from the Philistines, the tribe abandoned hopes of settling near the central coast and instead migrated to the north of the country. After conquering Laish, the tribe refounded it as its capital and renamed it [Dan](/wiki/Tel_Dan "Tel Dan"). In remembrance of the original territory assignments, the coastal region is referred to as Gush Dan. The modern city of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") was founded in 1909 as a suburb of the Arab-majority coastal city of [Jaffa](/wiki/Jaffa "Jaffa").[[9]](#cite_note-tel-aviv.gov.il-9)

The city of Tel Aviv grew rapidly in the ensuing decades by [Jewish immigration from Europe](/wiki/Immigration_to_Israel "Immigration to Israel"), with its population reaching 150,000 in 1934, and 230,000 when Israel gained its independence in 1948. Before the [establishment of Israel](/wiki/Establishment_of_Israel "Establishment of Israel"), other towns in the Gush Dan were founded as well, such as [Petah Tikva](/wiki/Petah_Tikva "Petah Tikva") in 1878, [Rishon LeZion](/wiki/Rishon_LeZion "Rishon LeZion") in 1882, [Ness Ziona](/wiki/Ness_Ziona "Ness Ziona") in 1883, [Rehovot](/wiki/Rehovot "Rehovot") in 1890, and most other Gush Dan cities were established before 1948.

In 1947, the Gush Dan had nearly 400,000 residents, comprising over half of the Jewish population of [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine"). As such, almost all of it was included in the Jewish state proposed by the 1947 [United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine"). After the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), the Arab population of the region, which had been nearly 150,000 before the war,[[9]](#cite_note-tel-aviv.gov.il-9) was reduced to around 10,000. Their population loss was quickly replaced by a larger number of Jews [fleeing from postwar Europe](/wiki/Emigration_of_Jews_from_Nazi_Germany_and_German-occupied_Europe#After_the_war "Emigration of Jews from Nazi Germany and German-occupied Europe") and [persecution in Arab countries](/wiki/Jewish_exodus_from_the_Muslim_world "Jewish exodus from the Muslim world").

However, many new immigrants did not then come to Tel Aviv. In the 1950s, towns were built on the edges of the Gush Dan, including [Ashdod](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod"), [Rosh HaAyin](/wiki/Rosh_HaAyin "Rosh HaAyin") and [Yavne](/wiki/Yavne "Yavne"). The nation's sole port was then located in the northern city of [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa") and its evolving metropolitan area, making that city at least as important as Tel Aviv. The new government was then trying to disperse the nation's population to the periphery and discouraged settlement in the already-populated Gush Dan. That slowed the growth of the Gush Dan, but the area still more than doubled in population within 20 years of the establishment of Israel. The opening of the [Port of Ashdod](/wiki/Port_of_Ashdod "Port of Ashdod") in the southern Gush Dan also increased the area's importance, with the importance of Haifa diminishing and that of Tel Aviv increasing because of its proximity. Tel Aviv itself witnessed population decreases in the 1970s and 1980s, when outer regions of the Gush Dan with lower costs of living absorbed many of the people who had left Tel Aviv.

Only in the 1990s, with the immigration of more than 1 million Jews [from former Soviet Republics](/wiki/1990s_post-Soviet_aliyah "1990s post-Soviet aliyah"), 40,000 [Ethiopian Jews](/wiki/Ethiopian_Jews "Ethiopian Jews"), and many others to Israel, as well as a boom in the religious population, would Tel Aviv begin to grow again. The demand for housing increased dramatically, with new cities such as [Modiin](/wiki/Modiin "Modiin") and [El'ad](/wiki/El%27ad "El'ad") being built, and cities like [Ashdod](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod") more than doubling in population, from 83,000 in 1990, to 175,000 in 2000. In the 2000s, the area continued to grow, attracting many immigrants from the [Haifa metropolitan area](/wiki/Haifa_metropolitan_area "Haifa metropolitan area"). Today Gush Dan is the commercial, economical, cultural, and industrial center of [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The Tel Aviv urban conurbation, stretching from Netanya to Ashdod, concentrates the largest Jewish population globally, nearing 3.9 million Jews. This figure significantly surpasses the Jewish population of the New York metropolitan area, which ranks second with 2.1 million Jews.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Despite some successes in ongoing attempts by the Israeli government to encourage migration to the [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") and the [Negev](/wiki/Negev "Negev"), Gush Dan retains its position as the heart of Israel.

* [Diamond Exchange District](/wiki/Diamond_Exchange_District "Diamond Exchange District") – [Ramat Gan](/wiki/Ramat_Gan "Ramat Gan") – The Diamond Exchange District is in the [city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Israel "List of cities in Israel") of Ramat Gan. Bordering the [Ayalon Highway](/wiki/Ayalon_Highway "Ayalon Highway"), the road dividing Ramat Gan and [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"), the district is home to [Israel's diamond industry](/wiki/Diamond_industry_in_Israel "Diamond industry in Israel") as well as being a major commercial center. The Diamond Exchange itself contains four buildings (towers) connected by bridges; the [Maccabi Tower](/w/index.php?title=Maccabi_Tower&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maccabi Tower (page does not exist)"), [Shimshon Tower](/w/index.php?title=Shimshon_Tower&action=edit&redlink=1 "Shimshon Tower (page does not exist)"), [Noam Tower](/w/index.php?title=Noam_Tower&action=edit&redlink=1 "Noam Tower (page does not exist)"), and [Diamond Tower](/wiki/Diamond_Tower "Diamond Tower"), which contains the world's largest diamond trading floor and is the main building of the Diamond Exchange. The district also has a number of other important buildings. The [Moshe Aviv Tower](/wiki/Moshe_Aviv_Tower "Moshe Aviv Tower") is Israel's second tallest (and formerly its tallest) building at 244 meters. Opposite, [Exchange Ramat Gan](/wiki/Exchange_Ramat_Gan "Exchange Ramat Gan") is currently under construction, set to include a pair of skyscrapers and a commercial area between them. The [Sheraton City Tower](/wiki/Sheraton_City_Tower "Sheraton City Tower") is a hotel in the district, whilst other notable buildings are the [Ayalon Tower](/wiki/Ayalon_Tower "Ayalon Tower") and [Gibor Sport House](/wiki/Gibor_Sport_House "Gibor Sport House").
* [Dizengoff Square](/wiki/Dizengoff_Square "Dizengoff Square") – Tel Aviv – [Dizengoff Center](/wiki/Dizengoff_Center "Dizengoff Center") ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): דיזנגוף סנטר) is a [shopping mall](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall") in central Tel Aviv, host to about 140,000 visitors weekly. Lying south of Dizengoff Square, it is named for [Meir Dizengoff](/wiki/Meir_Dizengoff "Meir Dizengoff"), the first mayor of Tel Aviv. The first mall in Tel Aviv, the center opened in 1983. It is divided into two parts and straddles both sides of [Dizengoff Street](/wiki/Dizengoff_Street "Dizengoff Street") with the two parts linked by a pair of skywalks. The mall is bordered by Dizengoff Street, [King George Street](/wiki/King_George_Street_(Tel_Aviv) "King George Street (Tel Aviv)") and the smaller Tchernichovsky street.
* [Port of Ashdod](/wiki/Port_of_Ashdod "Port of Ashdod") – [Ashdod](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod") – The Port of Ashdod is one of Israel's two main cargo [ports](/wiki/Port "Port"). The port is located in Ashdod, about 40 kilometers south of Tel Aviv, adjoining the mouth of the [Lachish River](/wiki/Lachish_River "Lachish River"). Its establishment doubled the country's port capacity.
* [Rothschild Boulevard](/wiki/Rothschild_Boulevard "Rothschild Boulevard") – Tel Aviv – Rothschild Boulevard ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): שְׂדֵרוֹת רוטשילד, *Sderot Rothschild*) is a street in Tel Aviv beginning in [Neve Tzedek](/wiki/Neve_Tzedek "Neve Tzedek") at its southwestern edge and running north to [Habima Theatre](/wiki/Habima_Theatre "Habima Theatre"). It is one of the busiest and most expensive streets in the Gush Dan, being one of the city's main tourist attractions.[[15]](#cite_note-Haaretz-15)
* [Azrieli Center](/wiki/Azrieli_Center "Azrieli Center") – Tel Aviv – Azrieli Center is a complex of [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscrapers "Skyscrapers") in Tel Aviv. At the base of the center lies a large [shopping mall](/wiki/Shopping_mall "Shopping mall"). The center was originally designed by Israeli-American architect Eli Attia, and after he fell out with the developer of the center [David Azrieli](/wiki/David_Azrieli "David Azrieli") (after whom it is named), completion of the design was passed on to the Tel Aviv firm of [Moore Yaski Sivan Architects](/wiki/Moore_Yaski_Sivan_Architects "Moore Yaski Sivan Architects").
* [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/BursaTelAviv-2.jpg/250px-BursaTelAviv-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:BursaTelAviv-2.jpg)

  The new Tel Aviv Stock Exchange

  [Tel Aviv Stock Exchange](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_Stock_Exchange "Tel Aviv Stock Exchange") – Tel Aviv – The Tel Aviv Stock Exchange (TASE; [Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): הבורסה לניירות ערך בתל אביב; colloquially known as the *Boursa*) is Israel's only [stock exchange](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange"). The TASE is the only [public market](/wiki/Financial_market "Financial market") for trading securities in Israel. It plays a major role in the [Israeli economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Israel "Economy of Israel"). TASE lists some 622 companies, about 60 of which are also listed on stock exchanges in other countries. TASE also lists some 180 [exchange-traded funds](/wiki/Exchange-traded_fund "Exchange-traded fund") (ETFs), 60 [government bonds](/wiki/Government_bond "Government bond"), 500 [corporate bonds](/wiki/Corporate_bond "Corporate bond"), and more than 1000 [mutual funds](/wiki/Mutual_fund "Mutual fund"). 29 companies are members of the TASE, of which 14 are banks. The [list of members](https://web.archive.org/web/20081014235855/http://www.tase.co.il/TASEEng/AboutTASE/TASEMembers/ContactDetails/) indicates that Altshuler Shaham Ltd is a candidate for membership.
* [Tel Aviv Promenade](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_Promenade "Tel Aviv Promenade") – Tel Aviv – The Tel Aviv promenade runs along its beaches, and is an integral part of the city's lifestyle, as well as a major tourist attraction. Most of the city's bathing beaches and hiking paths are concentrated in the central part of its 14 kilometers of Mediterranean shore. It contains numerous hotels and commercial buildings.
* [Kiryat Atidim](/wiki/Kiryat_Atidim "Kiryat Atidim") – Tel Aviv – high tech center in eastern [Ramat HaHayal](/wiki/Ramat_HaHayal "Ramat HaHayal"). The district is known for its ultra modern architecture.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gush_Dan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Littleuni_13.jpg/250px-Littleuni_13.jpg)](/wiki/File:Littleuni_13.jpg)

[Ayalon Highway](/wiki/Highway_20_(Israel) "Highway 20 (Israel)") separates Tel Aviv and Ramat Gan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/BenGurionAirportfromAir.jpg/250px-BenGurionAirportfromAir.jpg)](/wiki/File:BenGurionAirportfromAir.jpg)

[Ben Gurion International Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_International_Airport "Ben Gurion International Airport")

The [Dan Bus Company](/wiki/Dan_Bus_Company "Dan Bus Company") is primarily focused on serving the Gush Dan, although it is being replaced by the [Kavim](/wiki/Kavim "Kavim") company in many of the Gush Dan's cities. Much of Israel's [national highway network](/wiki/Roads_in_Israel "Roads in Israel") feeds into the area, such as [Highway 1](/wiki/Highway_1_(Israel) "Highway 1 (Israel)"), [Highway 2](/wiki/Highway_2_(Israel) "Highway 2 (Israel)"), [Highway 4](/wiki/Highway_4_(Israel) "Highway 4 (Israel)"), and [Highway 5](/wiki/Highway_5_(Israel) "Highway 5 (Israel)"). Gush Dan is also served by the local [Ayalon Highway](/wiki/Ayalon_Highway "Ayalon Highway"). [Israel Railways](/wiki/Israel_Railways "Israel Railways"), the state owned, national rail network provider, also feeds most traffic into or within the Gush Dan region. The [Tel Aviv Light Rail](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_Light_Rail "Tel Aviv Light Rail") is also a major feature in the regions transport, as well as the high speed service to [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"). Two airports are located in the Gush Dan; [Sde Dov Airport](/wiki/Sde_Dov_Airport "Sde Dov Airport") which closed at 2019,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and [Ben Gurion International Airport](/wiki/Ben_Gurion_International_Airport "Ben Gurion International Airport") in [Lod](/wiki/Lod "Lod") which is [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel")'s largest airport handles over 22 million passengers a year and offers flights to destinations in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), and The [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"). The [Tel Aviv Metro](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_Metro "Tel Aviv Metro") is a planned subway system to the region which will feature three lines, with the first public opening planned in 2032.

## Highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Gush_Dan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Highways")]

Some of the major freeways/expressways carrying commuter traffic in and out of the Tel Aviv Metropolitan Area are:

* **[Highway 20](/wiki/Highway_20_(Israel) "Highway 20 (Israel)")** (also called **Ayalon Highway**) – a major intracity [freeway](/wiki/Freeway "Freeway") in Gush Dan, which runs along Tel Aviv's center eastern border from north to south.
* **[Highway 1](/wiki/Highway_1_(Israel) "Highway 1 (Israel)")** – connects Tel Aviv with [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem").
* **[Highway 2](/wiki/Highway_2_(Israel) "Highway 2 (Israel)")** (also called **The Coastal Highway**) – stretches from Tel Aviv to [Haifa](/wiki/Haifa "Haifa"). It is one of the busiest highways in Israel.
* **[Highway 4](/wiki/Highway_4_(Israel) "Highway 4 (Israel)")** (also called **Geha Highway**, or **First President Road**) – a major north–south highway connecting [Ra'anana](/wiki/Ra%27anana "Ra'anana") and [Kfar Saba](/wiki/Kfar_Saba "Kfar Saba") in the North to [Petah Tikva](/wiki/Petah_Tikva "Petah Tikva") and [Ramat Gan](/wiki/Ramat_Gan "Ramat Gan") in the center and [Ashdod](/wiki/Ashdod "Ashdod") in the South.
* **[Highway 5](/wiki/Highway_5_(Israel) "Highway 5 (Israel)")** – connects the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") coast immediately north of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv") with the central [Sharon plain](/wiki/Sharon_plain "Sharon plain") and [Ariel](/wiki/Ariel_(city) "Ariel (city)") and other [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") in the northern [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank").
* **[Highway 44](/wiki/Highway_44_(Israel) "Highway 44 (Israel)")** – connects Tel Aviv with [Ramla](/wiki/Ramla "Ramla"), [Lod](/wiki/Lod "Lod") and the [Shefela](/wiki/Shefela "Shefela").
* **[Highway 6](/wiki/Highway_6_(Israel) "Highway 6 (Israel)")** – a new north–south [tollway](/wiki/Tollway "Tollway") running east of Gush Dan from [Galilee](/wiki/Galilee "Galilee") in the north to [Beer Sheva](/wiki/Beer_Sheva "Beer Sheva") in the south.
